summary: American silent film actor and director (1895–1966), known as the 'Great Stone Face' for his deadpan expression. Major films include The General, Steamboat Bill Jr., and Sherlock Jr. Known for performing his own dangerous stunts. Gilles Deleuze analyzed his use of 'trajectories' and 'machines.' Made his debut alongside Fatty Arbuckle in The Butcher Boy.

works.name: Steamboat Bill Jr. (1928)
works.description: The most famous scene: a cyclone causes the facade of a house to fall around Keaton's character, who miraculously survives because of the placement of an open window — his shoes were supposedly nailed to the ground so he wouldn't be tempted to move. His character (William 'Steamboat Bill' Canfield, owner of the Stonewall Jackson) wakes up to find his hospital bed exposed to the elements by a windstorm just before the scene. After the house facade, the protagonist unsuccessfully tries to cross a street and has boxes fall on his head. Dana Stevens's 2022 biography Cameraman connects the stunt metaphorically to the hurricane going through Keaton's career. Steve McQueen's 1997 film Deadpan re-creates the stunt. Wikipedia

clues.clue: A house facade falls around Keaton's character, who survives because of the placement of an open window; shoes were nailed to the ground so he wouldn't move

works.name: The General (1926)
works.description: Keaton plays Johnnie Gray / Johnnie Grey, a Civil War engineer who pursues Union army spies (who stole his locomotive, The General) in the locomotive The Texas. His girlfriend Annabelle Lee is smuggled by Johnnie in a burlap sack full of shoes. The film ends with Johnnie embracing Annabelle with his left hand while saluting passing soldiers with his right hand. A bridge (railroad trestle) collapses under the weight of The Texas. Captain Anderson is the villain. Wikipedia

works.name: Sherlock Jr. (1924)
works.description: Keaton plays a film projectionist. A villain called 'the local sheik' blames the theft of a pocket watch on Keaton's character. In a dream sequence, the protagonist falls asleep and enters the movie he's running. Keaton uses a series of pool trick shots to avoid hitting an explosive number 13 ball (having already replaced it). The film projectionist changes the price on a box of chocolates to seem more impressive. Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Buster Keaton (1895–1966), known as the 'Great Stone Face' for his deadpan expression. Always did his own stunts. Debut role was in The Butcher Boy alongside Fatty Arbuckle. Coached Lucille Ball in a scene for a TV pilot (instrumental in the creation of I Love Lucy). Gilles Deleuze argued his frequent use of 'trajectories' and 'machines' creates a unique link between a small hero and a giant situation. One film had him chased around a ship's deck by a miniature cannon and stuck in the wheel of a steam ship (The Navigator). In a film, he mimed a baseball game alone after showing up to film the Yankees on a day they were playing away. Dana Stevens wrote a 2022 biography Cameraman about him subtitled 'and the invention of the twentieth century.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Known as the 'Great Stone Face' for his deadpan expression; always did his own stunts

examples: This actor coached Lucille Ball in a scene from a TV pilot starring Ball as a dimwitted cellist which, though it wasn't aired at the time, was instrumental in the creation of I Love Lucy.

clues.clue: Gilles Deleuze argued his frequent use of 'trajectories' and 'machines' creates a unique link between a small hero and a giant situation
examples: Gilles Deleuze argued that this actor's frequent uses of 'trajectories' and 'machines' create a unique link between a small hero and a giant situation.

clues.clue: Dana Stevens's 2022 biography is titled Cameraman, subtitled '[Keaton] and the invention of the twentieth century'

comprehensive_summary:
Buster Keaton (1895–1966) was an American silent film actor and director known as the 'Great Stone Face' for his trademark deadpan expression; he always performed his own stunts. He made his debut in The Butcher Boy alongside Fatty Arbuckle. Gilles Deleuze analyzed his films, arguing that his frequent use of 'trajectories' and 'machines' creates a unique link between a small hero and a giant situation. Dana Stevens wrote a 2022 biography titled Cameraman ('and the invention of the twentieth century'). He also coached Lucille Ball in a scene for an unaired TV pilot that was instrumental in the creation of I Love Lucy.

Steamboat Bill Jr. (1928) is home to his most iconic stunt: a cyclone causes the facade of a house to fall around his character, who survives because of the placement of an open window — his shoes were supposedly nailed to the ground so he wouldn't be tempted to move. Just before the house scene, his character (the owner of the Stonewall Jackson) wakes up in a hospital to find his bed exposed to the elements. After the house falls, the protagonist unsuccessfully tries to cross a street and has boxes fall on his head. Dana Stevens connects this stunt metaphorically to the hurricane going through Keaton's career. Steve McQueen's 1997 film Deadpan re-creates the stunt (the title 'Deadpan' references Keaton's persona).

The General (1926) features Keaton as Johnnie Gray, a Civil War engineer who pursues Union army spies (who stole his locomotive The General) in the locomotive The Texas. He smuggles his girlfriend Annabelle Lee in a burlap sack full of shoes. The film ends with Johnnie embracing Annabelle with his left hand while saluting passing soldiers with his right. A famous scene shows a bridge collapsing under the weight of The Texas.

Sherlock Jr. (1924) features Keaton as a film projectionist; a villain called 'the local sheik' blames the theft of a pocket watch on him. In a dream sequence, the protagonist falls asleep and enters the movie he's projecting. He uses trick pool shots to avoid an explosive number 13 ball (having already replaced it).
